Ground Report: A mini-Tehran thrives in the heart of Tel AvivIndia Today's Pranay Upadhyaya reports from Levinsky Market in southern Tel Aviv, where Iranian heritage is on proud display in the midst of a war. Dubbed a "mini-Tehran", the market hosts dozens of spice shops run by Jewish immigrants from Iran who arrived after the Iranian Revolution. Vendors display saffron, dried herbs and traditional blends reminiscent of Tehran's bazaars and even India's old markets. Shopkeepers expressed concern over the ongoing hostilities, but said the people of Iran weren't to blame for its leadership's decisions. Some voiced hope for a future reconciliation. Despite political divides, the market reflects a shared cultural identity sustained through food, memory and migration.

India now has a nuclear reactor that makes more fuel than it burns. Here's howIndia's Prototype Fast Breeder Reactor at Kalpakkam has attained a historic milestone. The 500 MWe reactor, which produces more fuel than it consumes, puts India on course to harness its vast thorium reserves.

Live: Not every issue is gender-based, government tells Supreme CourtSabarimala SC Hearing Live Updates: A nine-judge bench of the Supreme Court will begin final hearings from April 7 on petitions concerning discrimination against women at religious places, including the Sabarimala temple, and the scope of religious freedom. The Constitution bench is led by Chief Justice Surya Kant. The case stems from a 2018 verdict, in which the top court, by a 4-1 majority, allowed entry of women aged 10 to 50 into the Sabarimala shrine, calling the ban unconstitutional. In 2019, a separate five-judge bench referred broader questions on women's entry and religious freedom across faiths to a larger bench for consideration. The Centre and the Kerala government have backed pleas for a review of the 2018 verdict. Stay with Indiatoday.in for the latest updates.

Live: Pakistan mediation efforts reach critical stage, says Iranian envoyUS-Israel-Iran War Live Updates: US President Donald Trump has escalated tensions with Iran, warning that "power plants and bridges" could be targeted if the Strait of Hormuz is not reopened by Tuesday evening, saying "Tuesday will be Power Plant Day, and Bridge Day, all wrapped up in one." Trump's abusive threat drew a sharp from Iranian parliament speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, who warned that Trump's "reckless moves" risk dragging the country into living hell and insisting "you won't gain anything through war crimes." Behind the scenes, Oman has held urgent talks with Iran to ensure smooth flow of transit through war-hit Hormuz, while Pakistan and Egypt have kept diplomatic channels open between Washington and Tehran. At the same time, violence has intensified elsewhere, with Israeli strikes in Beirut killing at least four people and Hezbollah launching retaliatory attacks, escalating the conflict further. Follow IndiaToday.in for the latest updates.
